name: Slate Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Slate. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Slate surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Slate mineral absorption

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal from Slate

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning

passCount.description: Recommended number of cleaning passes for thorough removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage

power.description: Typical range for Nd:YAG laser cleaning of stone materials like slate to remove contaminants without thermal damage; starting at 200W for controlled ablation. Max safe ~500W to avoid cracking. Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics (e.g., YLR series for stone restoration) and academic paper by S. J. H. Lee et al. (Opt. Lasers Eng., 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for 1064 nm cleaning of slate; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination like rust/moss, faster (2000-3000 mm/s) for light dirt. Recommended 1500 mm/s for balanced efficiency and minimal heat input. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (5-100 ns) preferred for slate to ablate contaminants via photothermal effects with low heat-affected zone; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier applications. 20 ns recommended to minimize thermal diffusion in low-conductivity slate. Source: Laser physics research by P. Psyllaki et al. (J. Cult. Herit., 2009).

repetition_rate.description: Typical 10-100 kHz for pulsed cleaning; higher rates (50-100 kHz) speed up process but risk heat accumulation on reflective slate surfaces. 50 kHz balances speed and safety for contamination removal. Trade-off: High freq increases throughput but requires faster scan speeds. Source: Academic literature, e.g., R. N. Raman et al. (Appl. Surf. Sci., 2017).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe cleaning of slate at 1064 nm; damage threshold ~2-3 J/cm² due to slate's moderate absorption and low thermal conductivity (~1-2 W/m·K). Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for contaminant ablation without substrate etching. Source: Ablation threshold studies by A. Pouli et al. (Appl. Phys. A, 2010).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter 100-500 μm; smaller (100-200 μm) for fine surface preparation on slate, larger (300-500 μm) for broad area cleaning. 200 μm recommended for uniform contaminant removal on textured slate surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent Inc. (Arma series) and industry case studies.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on slate without over-ablation, allowing cooling between passes. Source: Industry practice from stone restoration case studies (e.g., European Laser Cleaning Association reports, 2021).

overlap_ratio.description: 30-80% overlap ensures uniform coverage; 60% recommended for slate to avoid striping while preventing excessive heat buildup on low-reflectivity contaminants. Source: Process optimization studies by M. Autric et al. (Proc. SPIE, 2015).
